title: Long-run unemployment and real wage growth across the OECD
subtitle: Unit 16 'Technological progress, employment, and living standards in the long run’ Section 16.8 'Institutions and policies: Why do some countries do better than others?' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/16081220 [Figure 16.12]
note: Real wage growth is displayed on the 'MAP' tab. CC-BY-ND-NC
caption:
Long-run unemployment and real wage growth across the OECD

Unit 16 'Technological progress, employment, and living standards in the long run’ Section 16.8 'Institutions and policies: Why do some countries do better than others?' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/16081220 [Figure 16.12]

This scatter plot displays the relationship between average annual unemployment rates from 1970 to 2017 and average annual real wage growth from 1970 to 2011 across selected OECD countries. The horizontal axis ranges from 0% to 14% unemployment, while the vertical axis spans from 0% to 3% wage growth. Two diagonal dotted lines divide the chart into three performance zones labeled "Hi-Performers," "Middle Performers," and "Low Performers." Spain sits at the far right with an unemployment rate near 14.5% and wage growth around 1.1%. The United States and Canada occupy the lower-middle section, with unemployment between 6% and 8% and wage growth below 1.5%. A cluster of countries including Germany, France, the United Kingdom, and Norway appears in the upper-left quadrant, showing unemployment rates between 3% and 7.5% and wage growth between 2.5% and 3%. Japan and Sweden fall slightly below this group, with wage growth near 2% and unemployment under 6%. Belgium and the Netherlands sit closer to the middle performer boundary, with unemployment around 9% and 6% respectively and wage growth between 1.8% and 2.4%. The visual arrangement shows no single linear trend; instead, countries distribute across all three performance bands, with some achieving high wage growth alongside low unemployment, while others exhibit high unemployment with moderate or low wage growth.
